en.extract:
The pied butcherbird (Cracticus nigrogularis) is a songbird native to Australia. Initially described by John Gould in 1837, it is a black-and-white bird 28 to 32 cm (11 to 12.5 in) long with a long, hooked bill. Its head and throat are black, making a distinctive hood; the mantle and much of the tail and wings are also black. The neck, underparts, and outer wing feathers are white. The juvenile and immature birds are predominantly brown and white. As they mature, black feathers replace their brown feathers. There are two recognised subspecies of pied butcherbird.
Within its range, the pied butcherbird is generally sedentary. Common in woodlands and urban environments, it is carnivorous, eating insects and small vertebrates, including birds. A tame and inquisitive bird, the pied butcherbird has been known to accept food from humans. It nests in trees, constructing a cup-shaped structure out of sticks and laying two to five eggs. The pied butcherbird engages in cooperative breeding, with a mated pair sometimes assisted by several helper birds. The troop is territorial, defending the nesting site from intruders. The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has assessed the pied butcherbird as being of least concern on account of its large range and apparently stable population.


Taxonomy

The pied butcherbird was first described by the ornithologist John Gould in 1837 as Vanga nigrogularis. The type specimen was collected near Sydney. The species name is from the Latin words niger (black) and gula (throat). Gould described Cracticus picatus in 1848 from northern Australia, calling it "[a] miniature representative of, and nearly allied to, but distinct from, Cracticus nigrogularis." The word picatus is Latin for "daubed with pitch", hence "black patches". This was reclassified as a subspecies of C. nigrogularis. Gregory Mathews described subspecies inkermani from Queensland and subspecies mellori from Victoria and South Australia in 1912, based on smaller and larger sizes than the nominate subspecies, respectively. Both are now regarded as inseparable from the nominate subspecies. Mathews described subspecies kalgoorli from Kalgoorlie in 1912 on the basis of its longer bill than the nominate subspecies, but it is regarded today as part of subspecies picatus.
Two subspecies are recognised today. The nominate subspecies nigrogularis is found across eastern Australia, and subspecies picatus is found in the Northern Territory, Western Australia, and northern South Australia. The latter subspecies has a broader (3.7 cm (1.5 in) wide) white collar and a more whitish rump, with specimens becoming smaller in the more northern parts of the range. The border between the two subspecies lies in the Gulf Country and is known as the Carpentarian Barrier. Although there is a demarcation in physical characters, this is not borne out genetically, and birds from northwestern Australia have affinities with the eastern subspecies. Analysis of mitochondrial DNA sequences indicates the pied butcherbird has expanded rapidly from many refugia during the Pleistocene.
The pied butcherbird is one of six (or seven) members of the genus Cracticus, known colloquially as butcherbirds. Within the genus, it is most closely related to the Tagula butcherbird (C. louisiadensis) and hooded butcherbird (C. cassicus). The three form a monophyletic group within the genus, having diverged from ancestors of the grey butcherbird around five million years ago. The butcherbirds, Australian magpie (Gymnorhina tibicen), and currawongs (Strepera spp.) were placed in the family Cracticidae in 1914 by John Albert Leach after he had studied their musculature. American ornithologists Charles Sibley and Jon Ahlquist recognised the close relationship between woodswallows and the butcherbirds in 1985 and combined them into a Cracticini clade, which became the family Artamidae in 1994.
"Pied butcherbird" has been designated the official English name by the International Ornithological Committee (IOC). "Black-throated butcherbird" is an alternative common name, as are "Break o'day boy" and "organbird". Leach also called it the "black-throated crow-shrike", a name used by Gould for subspecies nigrogularis while calling subspecies picatus the "pied crow-shrike". 'Jackeroo' is a colloquial name from the Musgrave Ranges in Central Australia. Gould recorded Ka-ra-a-ra as a name used by indigenous people of Darwin. The Ngarluma people of the western Pilbara knew it as gurrbaru. In the Yuwaaliyaay dialect of the Gamilaraay language of southeastern Australia, it is buubuurrbu. Names recorded from central Australia include alpirtaka and urbura in the Upper Arrernte language.


Description
 
Like other butcherbirds, the pied butcherbird is stockily built with short legs and a relatively large head. It ranges from 28 to 32 cm (11 to 12.5 in) long, averaging around 31 cm (12 in), with a 51 cm (20 in) wingspan and a weight of around 120 g (4 oz). The wings are fairly long, extending to halfway along the tail when folded. Its plumage is almost wholly black and white, with very little difference between the sexes. It has a black head, nape, and throat, giving it the appearance of a black hood, which is bounded by a white neck collar, which is around 3.2 cm (1.2 in) wide. The black hood is slightly glossy in bright light, can fade a little with age, and is slightly duller and more brownish in the adult female. The neck collar in the female is slightly narrower at around 2.5 cm (1 in) and is a grey-white rather than white. Several stiff black bristles up to 1.5 cm (0.5 in) long arise from the lower lores. The upper mantle and a few of the front scapulars are white, contrasting sharply with the black lower mantle and the rest of the scapulars. The rump is pale grey, and the upper tail coverts are white. The tail is rather long, with a rounded or wedge-shaped tip. It has twelve rectrices, which are black in colour. The tail tip and outer wing feathers are white, as well as the underparts. The eyes are a dark brown, the legs are grey, and the bill is a pale bluish grey tipped with black, with a prominent hook at the end.
The juvenile pied butcherbird has dark brown instead of black plumage, lacks the pale collar, and has cream-to-buff lores, chin, and upper throat, becoming more brown on the lower throat and breast. Its underparts are off-white to cream, and the bill is dark brown. In its first year, it moults into its first immature plumage, which resembles that of the juvenile but has a more extensive dark brown throat. Its bill is blue-grey with a dark brown or blackish tip.


Voice

The pied butcherbird has been considered the most accomplished songbird in Australia, its song described as a "magic flute" by one writer, richer and clearer than the Australian magpie. Song melodies vary across the continent, and no single song is sung by the whole population. There is no clear demarcation between simple calls and elaborate songs: duets and even larger choirs are common. The species improvises extensively in creating new and complex melodies. One of its calls has been likened to the opening bars of Beethoven's Fifth Symphony. Singing often takes place at dawn and rarely late in the day. Pied butcherbirds sometimes sing on moonlit nights.
Three types of song have been described: the day song is the most common, sung by birds alone or in pairs as a chorus or an antiphonal duet, generally over the course of the day and while the birds are in flight. It appears to promote bonding and act as communication. The whisper song is sung more commonly in wet or windy weather, the singer sitting in a tree warbling soft and complex harmonies for up to 45 minutes, often mimicking many other bird species as well as dogs barking, lambs bleating, or even people whistling. In the breeding season, pied butcherbirds sing the breeding song at night until dawn, when they switch to the day song. This song is longer and more complex than the day song. In response to threats, pied butcherbirds may chatter or make a harmonic alarm call composed of short, loud, descending notes.


Similar species
The black hood helps distinguish the pied butcherbird from other butcherbirds, the Australian magpie, and the much smaller magpie-lark, the latter of which also has a much smaller beak. It has a higher-pitched call than the grey butcherbird and inhabits more open habitat. The juvenile pied butcherbird resembles the grey butcherbird: it has a buff upper throat and dark brown instead of black plumage.


Distribution and habitat

The pied butcherbird is found across much of Australia, except for the far south of the mainland and Tasmania. It is only rarely recorded in the Sydney Basin and absent from the Illawarra, Southern Tablelands, and south coast of New South Wales. In Victoria, it is found along the Murray Valley and west of Chiltern. In South Australia, it is not found in the north-east of the state nor on the Adelaide plain. It is found across Western Australia, though it is absent from the Great Sandy Desert. It is generally sedentary across most of its range, with minimal seasonal movements.
It is a bird of open sclerophyll forests, eucalypt and acacia woodlands, and scrublands with sparse or no understory or low cover with shrubs such as Triodia, Lomandra, or Hibbertia. It is less common in mallee scrub. In arid areas and northern Australia, it is more restricted to woodland alongside rivers and billabongs. It has become more common in south-west Western Australia with land clearing, though it has become rare around Darwin on account of urban development.


Conservation status
The pied butcherbird is listed as being a species of least concern by the IUCN on account of its large range and stable population, with no evidence of any significant decline.


Behaviour
The pied butcherbird is thought to be monogamous, though its breeding habits have not been studied much. There is evidence of cooperative breeding, with some mated pairs being assisted by up to several other helper birds. These individuals help feed the young and defend the nest. These pairs or small groups defend their territory from intruders, mobbing and chasing raptors and other birds, and occasionally dogs or people. They may attack animals (and people) that venture too close to the nest, with one bird coming front-on while the other may approach from behind.
The maximum age recorded from banding has been 22 years and 1.7 months for an individual banded in Rockhampton in June 1988 and recovered in August 2010 – 7 km (4.3 mi) away. The bird was injured and had to be euthanised.


Breeding

Across most of its range, the pied butcherbird can generally be found breeding from winter to summer; eggs are laid anywhere from July to December, but mostly from September to November, and young can be present in the nest from August to February. There are reports of breeding outside these months, however. The nest is constructed of dry sticks with a finer material such as dried grass, black roly poly (Sclerolaena muricata), bark, and leaves forming a cup-shaped interior. It is located in the fork of a tree, often among foliage, and inconspicuously. The clutch consists of two to five (most commonly three or four) oval eggs blotched with brown over a base colour of various shades of pale greyish- or brownish-green. Larger clutches have been recorded, such as at Jandowae in Queensland, where two pairs laid eggs and were sharing incubation duties. Eggs of subspecies nigrogularis are larger, at around 33 mm long by 24 mm (1.3 by 0.95 in) wide, while those of subspecies picatus are around 31 mm long by 22 mm (1.2 by 0.85 in) wide. Incubation takes 19 to 21 days, with the eggs laid up to 48 hours apart and hatching at a similar interval. As with all passerines, the chicks are altricial—they are born naked or sparsely covered in down and blind. They spend anywhere from 25 to 33 days in the nest before fledging, though they may leave the nest early if disturbed. They are fed by their parents and helper birds. Brood parasites recorded include the pallid cuckoo (Cacomantis pallidus) and channel-billed cuckoo (Scythrops novaehollandiae).


Feeding
The pied butcherbird is carnivorous and eats insects such as beetles, bugs, ants, caterpillars, and cockroaches, as well as spiders and worms. It preys on vertebrates up to the size of animals such as frogs, skinks, mice, and small birds such as the silvereye (Zosterops lateralis), house sparrow (Passer domesticus), double-barred finch (Taeniopygia bichenovii), willie wagtail (Rhipidura leucophrys), and grey teal (Anas gracilis) duckling. It has been looked upon favourably by farmers, since it hunts such pests as grasshoppers and rodents. Some individuals look for scraps around houses and picnic sites and can become tame enough to be fed by people, either by hand or by tossing food in the air. The pied butcherbird also eats fruit, such as those of sandpaper figs (Ficus coronata), native cherry (Exocarpos cupressiformis), African boxthorn (Lycium ferocissimum), and grapes (Vitis vinifera), and nectar of the Darwin woollybutt (Eucalyptus miniata).
The pied butcherbird often perches on a fencepost, stump, or branch while foraging for prey. It generally pounces on victims on the ground and eats them there. At times, it may hop or run along hunting ground-based food and occasionally seize flying insects. It typically forages alone or occasionally in pairs. The pied butcherbird has been observed hunting collaboratively with the Australian hobby, either picking off common starlings or rufous-throated honeyeaters disturbed by the larger hobby or flushing out small birds from bushes, which the larger bird then hunts. The pied butcherbird sometimes stores food items by impaling them on a stick or on barbed wire or shoving them in a nook or crevice.


Cultural significance
Several Australian and international composers have been inspired by and written music incorporating the songs of the pied butcherbird, including Henry Tate, David Lumsdaine (who described it as "a virtuoso of composition and improvisation"), Don Harper, Olivier Messiaen, Elaine Barkin, John Rodgers, Ron Nagorcka, and John Williamson. In the dance "Bird Song" by Siobhan Davies, the main central solo was accompanied by the call of a pied butcherbird, and this same sound provided inspiration to much of the dance, including the improvisational aspects. Composer and researcher Hollis Taylor has studied pied butcherbird songs for 12 years and has released a double CD called Absolute Bird based on fifty-plus pied butcherbird nocturnal solo songs. Taylor's "Is Birdsong Music? Outback Encounters with an Australian Songbird" offers portraits of the remote locations where the species is found.
In the now extinct Warray language spoken on the Adelaide River in Arnhem Land, Cracticus nigrogularis was known as lopolopo.


References


Cited texts
Higgins, Peter Jeffrey; Peter, John M.; Cowling, S. J., eds. (2006). Handbook of Australian, New Zealand and Antarctic Birds. Vol. 7: Boatbill to Starlings. Melbourne, Victoria: Oxford University Press. ISBN 978-0-19-553996-7.


External links

Pied butcherbird videos, photos & sounds on the Macaulay Library

The '''pied butcherbird''' ('''''Cracticus nigrogularis''''') is a [[songbird]] native to [[Birds of Australia|Australia]]. Initially [[Species description|described]] by [[John Gould]] in 1837, it is a black-and-white bird {{convert|28|to|32|cm|in|round=0.5|abbr=on}} long with a long, hooked [[Beak|bill]]. Its head and throat are black, making a distinctive hood; the [[Bird#Anatomy and physiology|mantle]] and much of the tail and [[bird wing|wings]] are also black. The neck, underparts, and outer wing [[feather]]s are white. The juvenile and immature birds are predominantly brown and white. As they mature, black feathers replace their brown feathers. There are two recognised [[subspecies]] of pied butcherbird.

Within its [[Species distribution|range]], the pied butcherbird is generally sedentary. Common in [[woodland]]s and [[urban wildlife|urban environments]], it is carnivorous, [[insectivore|eating insects]] and small vertebrates, including birds. A tame and inquisitive bird, the pied butcherbird has been known to accept food from humans. It [[bird nest|nests]] in trees, constructing a cup-shaped structure out of sticks and laying two to five [[bird egg|eggs]]. The pied butcherbird engages in [[cooperative breeding]], with a mated pair sometimes assisted by several helper birds. The troop is [[Territory (animal)|territorial]], defending the nesting site from intruders. The [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] (IUCN) has assessed the pied butcherbird as being of [[Least-concern species|least concern]] on account of its large range and apparently stable population.

==Taxonomy==
[[File:Cracticus nigrogularis by John Gould.JPG|thumb|left|Painting by [[John Gould]]]]
The pied butcherbird was first described by the [[ornithologist]] John Gould in 1837 as ''Vanga nigrogularis''. The [[Type (biology)#In zoology|type specimen]] was collected near [[Sydney]].<ref name=Amadon51/><ref>{{cite book | last=Gould | first=John | author-link=John Gould | year=1837 | title=A Synopsis of previous 2 sentences --> The species name is from the [[Latin]] words {{Lang|la|niger}} (black) and {{Lang|la|gula}} (throat).<ref>{{cite book|author = Simpson, D.P.| title = Cassell's Latin Both are now regarded as inseparable from the nominate subspecies.<ref name="AFD">{{cite web|url=https://biodiversity.org.au/afd/taxa/Cracticus_nigrogularis_nigrogularis|title=Subspecies ''Cracticus Capital Territory}}</ref>

Two subspecies are recognised today. The nominate subspecies ''nigrogularis'' is found across eastern Australia,<ref name=Amadon51/> and subspecies ''picatus'' is found in the [[Northern Territory]], [[Western Australia]], and northern [[South Australia]].<ref name=kearns2010/> The latter subspecies has a broader ({{cvt|3.7|cm}} wide) white collar and a more whitish rump, with specimens becoming smaller in the more northern parts of the range.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=527}} The border between the two subspecies lies in the [[Gulf Country]] and is known as the Carpentarian Barrier. Although there is a demarcation in physical characters, this is not borne out genetically, and birds from northwestern Australia have affinities with the eastern subspecies. Analysis of [[mitochondrial DNA]] sequences indicates the pied butcherbird has expanded rapidly from many [[Refugium (population biology)|refugia]] during the [[Pleistocene]].<ref name=kearns2010>{{Cite journal | last1 = Kearns | first1 = Anna M. | last2 = Joseph | first2 = Leo | last3 = Cook | first3 = Lyn G. | doi = 10.1071/MU10020 | title = The impact of Pleistocene changes of climate and landscape on Australian birds: A test using the Pied Butcherbird (''Cracticus nigrogularis'') | journal = Emu | volume = 110 | issue = 4 | pages = 285–95 | year = 2010| bibcode = 2010EmuAO.110..285K | s2cid = 83505750 }}</ref>

The pied butcherbird is one of six (or seven) members of the genus ''[[Cracticus]]'', known colloquially as [[butcherbird]]s. Within the genus, it is most closely related to the [[Tagula butcherbird]] (''C. louisiadensis'') and [[hooded butcherbird]] (''C. cassicus''). The three form a monophyletic group within the genus, having diverged from ancestors of the [[grey butcherbird]] around five million years ago.<ref name="kearns13">{{cite journal|doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2012.11.020|author1=Kearns, Anna |author2=Joseph, Leo |author3=Cook, Lyn G. |year=2013|title=A multilocus coalescent analysis of the speciational history of the Australo-Papuan Butcherbirds and their allies | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution|volume=66|issue=3|pages=941–52 |pmid=23219707|bibcode=2013MolPE..66..941K }}</ref> The butcherbirds, [[Australian magpie]] (''Gymnorhina tibicen''), and [[currawong]]s (''Strepera'' spp.) were placed in the family Cracticidae in 1914 by [[John Albert Leach]] after he had studied their musculature.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1071/MU914002 |last=Leach |first=John Albert |author-link=John Albert Leach |year=1914 |title=The myology of the Bell-Magpie (''Strepera'') and its position in classification |journal=Emu |volume=14 |issue=1 |pages=2–38 |bibcode=1914EmuAO..14....2L |url=https://zenodo.org/record/1429902 |archive-date=2020-08-01 |access-date=2019-07-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200801101418/https://zenodo.org/record/1429902 |url-status=live }}</ref> American ornithologists [[Charles Sibley]] and [[Jon Edward Ahlquist|Jon Ahlquist]] recognised the close relationship between [[woodswallow]]s and the butcherbirds in 1985 and combined them into a Cracticini [[clade]],<ref name="SibAhl85">{{cite journal|doi=10.1071/MU9850001|last1=Sibley|first1=C.G.|last2=Ahlquist|first2=J.E.|year=1985|title=The phylogeny and classification of Australo-Papuan passerine birds|journal=Emu|volume=85|issue=1|pages=1–14|bibcode=1985EmuAO..85....1S|url=http://www.publish.csiro.au/?act=view_file&file_id=MU9850001.pdf|access-date=15 April 2009|archive-date=12 April 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160412002041/http://www.publish.csiro.au/?act=view_file&file_id=MU9850001.pdf|url-status=live}}</ref> which became the family Artamidae in 1994.<ref |isbn=978-0-643-06511-6 | page=196}}</ref>

"Pied butcherbird" has been designated the [[Birds of the World: Recommended English Names|official English name]] by the [[International Ornithological Committee]] (IOC).<ref name="ioclist">{{cite web | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | year=2017 | title=Bristlehead, Butcherbirds, Woodswallows & Cuckooshrikes | work=World Bird List Version 7.3 | url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/butcherbirds/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=5 September 2017 | archive-date=7 May 2014 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140507001345/http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/butcherbirds/ | url-status=live }}</ref> "Black-throated butcherbird" is an alternative common name, as are "Break o'day boy" and "organbird".{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=516}} Leach also called it the "black-throated crow-shrike",<ref>{{cite book | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Rn3uthhODo8C&pg=PA149 | title=An Australian Bird Book: A Complete Guide to the Identification of Australian Birds | author=Leach, J.A. | year=2005 | isbn=978-1-4179-8449-7| page=149 | publisher=Kessinger Publishing | location= Whitefish, Montana}}</ref> a name used by Gould for subspecies ''nigrogularis'' while calling subspecies ''picatus'' the "pied crow-shrike".<ref name="gould 1865">{{cite book|last=Gould|first=John|title=Handbook to The birds of Australia|publisher=self|location=London, United Kingdom|date=1865|pages=180–82|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8459748|archive-date=2017-10-11|access-date=2018-02-21|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171011080530/https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8459748|url-status=live}}</ref> 'Jackeroo' is a colloquial name from the [[Musgrave Ranges]] in [[Central Australia]].<ref>{{Cite journal | last1 = McGilp | first1 = J. Neil | title = Birds of the Musgrave Ranges | doi = 10.1071/MU934163 | journal = Emu | volume = 34 | issue = 3 | pages = 163–75 | year = 1934}}</ref> Gould recorded ''Ka-ra-a-ra'' as a name used by indigenous people of [[Darwin, Northern Territory|Darwin]].<ref name="gould 1865"/> The [[Ngarluma]] people of the western Pilbara knew it as ''gurrbaru''.<ref>{{Cite book | title = Ngarluma Dictionary:English-Ngarluma Wordlist and Topical Wordlists | year = 2008 | publisher = Wanga Maya Pilbara Aboriginal Language Centre | location = South Hedland, Western Australia | url = http://ngarluma.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2013/04/Ngarluma-Language-Dictionary.pdf | isbn = 978-1-921312-73-1 | page = 17 | access-date = 2017-09-05 | archive-date = 2017-10-11 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20171011080328/http://ngarluma.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2013/04/Ngarluma-Language-Dictionary.pdf }}</ref> In the Yuwaaliyaay dialect of the [[Gamilaraay language]] of southeastern Australia, it is {{Lang|kld|buubuurrbu}}.<ref>{{cite book | last=Giacon |first= Gianbattista (John) | year= 2013 | chapter=Etymology of Yuwaalaraay Gamilaraay bird names |editor= Robert Mailhammer | title= Lexical and Structural Etymology: Beyond Word Histories | publisher= Walter de Gruyter | location= Boston/Berlin | pages= 251–291 [268]}}</ref> Names recorded from central Australia include ''alpirtaka'' and ''urbura'' in the [[Upper Arrernte language]].<ref>{{cite journal | url=http://www.birdssa.asn.au/images/saopdfs/Volume21/1955V21P091.pdf | title=Aboriginal bird names - South Australia | last=Condon | first=H.T. | journal=S.A. Ornithologist | pages=91–98 | year=1955 | volume=21 | archive-date=2019-04-30 | access-date=2017-09-06 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190430134725/https://birdssa.asn.au/images/saopdfs/Volume21/1955V21P091.pdf | url-status=live }}</ref>

==Description==
[[File:Piedbutcherbirdgibsondesert.jpg|thumb|right|Subsp. ''picatus'' at Slate Range ([[Gibson Desert]])]] 
Like other butcherbirds, the pied butcherbird is stockily built with short legs and a relatively large head.<ref name=Amadon51>{{cite journal|author=Amadon, Dean|year=1951|title=Taxonomic notes on the Australian butcher-birds (family Cracticidae)|journal= American Museum Novitates|issue=1504|pages=1–33|hdl=2246/3960}}</ref> It ranges from {{convert|28|to|32|cm|in|round=0.5|abbr=on}} long, averaging around {{convert|31|cm|in|0|abbr=on}}, with a {{convert|51|cm|in|0|abbr=on}} wingspan and a weight of around {{convert|120|g|oz|0|abbr=on}}.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=516}} The wings are fairly long, extending to halfway along the tail when folded.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=527}} Its [[plumage]] is almost wholly black and white, with very little difference between the sexes.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=525}} It has a black head, nape, and throat, giving it the appearance of a black hood, which is bounded by a white neck collar,<ref name=bib>{{cite web|url=http://birdsinbackyards.net/species/Cracticus-nigrogularis|title=Pied Butcherbird (''Cracticus nigrogularis'')|work=Birds in Backyards|publisher=Australian Museum|location=Sydney, NSW|date=8 March 2008|access-date=16 December 2011|archive-date=9 December 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111209010343/http://birdsinbackyards.net/species/Cracticus-nigrogularis}}</ref> which is around 3.2 cm (1.2 in) wide. The black hood is slightly glossy in bright light, can fade a little with age,{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=524-25}} and is slightly [[sexual dimorphism|duller and more brownish]] in the adult female. The neck collar in the female is slightly narrower at around {{convert|2.5|cm|in|0|abbr=on}} and is a grey-white rather than white.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=525}} Several stiff black bristles up to 1.5 cm (0.5 in) long arise from the lower [[Lore (anatomy)|lores]]. The upper [[Bird#Anatomy and physiology|mantle]] and a few of the front [[List of terms used in bird topography#Plumage features|scapulars]] are white, contrasting sharply with the black lower mantle and the rest of the scapulars. The rump is pale grey, and the upper tail [[covert feather|coverts]] are white. The tail is rather long, with a rounded or wedge-shaped tip. It has twelve [[Flight feather#Rectrices|rectrices]],{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=527}}<!-- cites previous 1.5 sentences --> which are black in colour.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=525}} The tail tip and outer wing feathers are white, as well as the underparts. The eyes are a dark brown, the legs are grey, and the bill is a pale bluish grey tipped with black,<ref name=Slater74>{{cite book |title=A Field Guide to Australian Birds: Passerines |last=Slater |first=Peter|year=1974 |publisher=Rigby |location=Adelaide, South Australia |isbn=0-85179-813-6|page=278}}</ref> with a prominent hook at the end.<ref name=Amadon51/>

The juvenile pied butcherbird has dark brown instead of black plumage, lacks the pale collar, and has cream-to-buff lores, chin, and upper throat, becoming more brown on the lower throat and breast. Its underparts are off-white to cream,{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=525}} and the bill is dark brown.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=526}} In its first year, it [[Moulting#In birds|moults]] into its first immature plumage, which resembles that of the juvenile but has a more extensive dark brown throat. Its bill is blue-grey with a dark brown or blackish tip.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=526}}

===Voice===
[[File:Pied Butcherbird 2.jpg|thumb|left|Immature bird – City Botanic Gardens – Brisbane]]
The pied butcherbird has been considered the most accomplished songbird in Australia,<ref name=bib/> its [[bird vocalization|song]] described as a "magic flute" by one writer, richer and clearer than the Australian magpie.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1 = Hartshorne | first1 = Charles| title = Musical values in Australian bird songs | doi = 10.1071/MU953109 | journal = Emu | volume = 53 | issue = 2 | pages = 109–28 | year = 1953 | bibcode = 1953EmuAO..53..109H}}</ref> Song melodies vary across the continent, and no single song is sung by the whole population. There is no clear demarcation between simple calls and elaborate songs: duets and even larger choirs are common.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Taylor|first=Hollis|year=2008|title=Decoding the song of the pied butcherbird: an initial survey|journal=TRANS-Transcultural Music Review|volume=12|url=http://www.sibetrans.com/trans/a98/decoding-the-song-of-the-pied-butcherbird-an-initial-survey|access-date=23 September 2017|archive-date=26 June 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120626014708/http://www.sibetrans.com/trans/a98/decoding-the-song-of-the-pied-butcherbird-an-initial-survey|url-status=live}}</ref> The species improvises extensively in creating new and complex melodies.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Taylor|first=Hollis|year=2010|title=Blowin' in Birdland: Improvisation and the Australian Pied Butcherbird|journal=Leonardo Music Journal|volume=20|pages=79–83|doi=10.1162/lmj_a_00016|s2cid=55691878|url=https://zenodo.org/record/895424}}</ref> One of its calls has been likened to the opening bars of [[Symphony No. 5 (Beethoven)|Beethoven's Fifth Symphony]].<ref name=Slater74/> Singing often takes place at dawn and rarely late in the day. Pied butcherbirds sometimes sing on moonlit nights.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=522}}

Three types of song have been described: the ''day song'' is the most common, sung by birds alone or in pairs as a chorus or an [[antiphon]]al duet, generally over the course of the day and while the birds are in flight. It appears to promote bonding and act as communication.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=522}} The ''whisper song'' is sung more commonly in wet or windy weather, the singer sitting in a tree warbling soft and complex harmonies for up to 45 minutes, often mimicking many other bird species as well as dogs barking, lambs bleating, or even people whistling.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=523}} In the breeding season, pied butcherbirds sing the ''breeding song'' at night until dawn, when they switch to the day song. This song is longer and more complex than the day song.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=522}} In response to threats, pied butcherbirds may chatter or make a harmonic alarm call composed of short, loud, descending notes.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=523}}

===Similar species===
The black hood helps distinguish the pied butcherbird from other butcherbirds, the Australian magpie, and the much smaller magpie-lark, the latter of which also has a much smaller beak.<ref name=bib/> It has a higher-pitched call than the [[grey butcherbird]] and inhabits more open habitat.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=517}} The juvenile pied butcherbird resembles the grey butcherbird: it has a buff upper throat and dark brown instead of black plumage.<ref name=Slater74/>

==Distribution and habitat==
[[File:Pied butcherbird (Cracticus nigrogularis) on a tree stump at Bomen Lagoon.jpg|thumb|Bomen Lagoon, [[North Wagga Wagga]], New South Wales]]
The pied butcherbird is found across much of Australia, except for the far south of the mainland and [[Tasmania]].<ref name=bib/> It is only rarely recorded in the [[Sydney Basin]] and absent from the [[Illawarra]], [[Southern Tablelands]], and south coast of [[New South Wales]]. In Victoria, it is found along the [[Murray River|Murray Valley]] and west of [[Chiltern, Victoria|Chiltern]].{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=518}} In South Australia, it is not found in the north-east of the state nor on the Adelaide plain. It is found across Western Australia, though it is absent from the [[Great Sandy Desert]]. It is generally sedentary across most of its range, with minimal seasonal movements.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=519}}<!-- cites previous 2 sentences -->

It is a bird of open [[sclerophyll]] forests, [[eucalypt]] and [[acacia]] woodlands, and scrublands with sparse or no [[understory]] or low cover with shrubs such as ''[[Triodia (grass)|Triodia]]'', ''[[Lomandra]]'', or ''[[Hibbertia]]''. It is less common in [[Mallee Woodlands and Shrublands|mallee scrub]]. In arid areas and northern Australia, it is more restricted to woodland alongside rivers and [[billabong]]s.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=517}} It has become more common in south-west Western Australia with land clearing, though it has become rare around [[Darwin, Northern Territory|Darwin]] on account of urban development.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=519}}

===Conservation status===
The pied butcherbird is listed as being a [[least concern|species of least concern]] by the [[IUCN]] on account of its large range and stable population, with no evidence of any significant decline.<ref name="iucn status" />

==Behaviour==
The pied butcherbird is thought to be [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous]], though its [[Bird#Breeding|breeding]] habits have not been studied much. There is evidence of [[cooperative breeding]], with some mated pairs being assisted by up to several other helper birds. These individuals help feed the young and defend the nest.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=521}} These pairs or small groups defend their territory from intruders, mobbing and chasing raptors and other birds, and occasionally dogs or people. They may attack animals (and people) that venture too close to the nest, with one bird coming front-on while the other may approach from behind.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=521}}<!-- cites previous 2 sentences -->

The maximum age recorded from banding has been 22 years and 1.7 months for an individual banded in [[Rockhampton]] in June 1988 and recovered in August 2010 – {{cvt|7|km}} away. The bird was injured and had to be euthanised.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.environment.gov.au/cgi-bin/biodiversity/abbbs/abbbs-search.pl|title=ABBBS Database Search: ''Cracticus nigrogularis'' (Pied butcherbird)|last=Australian Bird & Bat Banding Scheme (ABBBS)|date=2017|work=Bird and bat banding database|publisher=Australian Government Department of the Environment, Water, Heritage and the Arts|access-date=26 August 2017|archive-date=15 March 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200315004238/http://www.environment.gov.au/topics/science-and-research/bird-and-bat-banding/banding-data/search-abbbs-database?NO_DATA=|url-status=live}}</ref>

===Breeding===
[[File:Pied Butcherbird rushck.ogv|thumb|right|Rush Creek, SE Queensland]]
Across most of its range, the pied butcherbird can generally be found breeding from winter to summer; eggs are laid anywhere from July to December, but mostly from September to November, and young can be present in the nest from August to February. There are reports of breeding outside these months, however.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=523}} The nest is constructed of dry sticks with a finer material such as dried grass, black roly poly (''[[Sclerolaena muricata]]''), bark, and leaves forming a cup-shaped interior. It is located in the fork of a tree, often among foliage, and inconspicuously.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=523}} The [[Clutch (eggs)|clutch]] consists of two to five (most commonly three or four) oval eggs blotched with brown over a base colour of various shades of pale greyish- or brownish-green.<ref name=Ber93>{{cite book  | last = Beruldsen  | first = Gordon  | author-link=Gordon Beruldsen|title = Australian Birds: Their Nests and Eggs  | publisher = self  | year = 2003  | location = Kenmore Hills, Queensland  | page = 373 |isbn = 0-646-42798-9}}</ref><!-- cites previous 2 sentences --> Larger clutches have been recorded, such as at [[Jandowae]] in Queensland, where two pairs laid eggs and were sharing incubation duties.<ref>{{cite journal | title= Double clutch of Pied Butcher-bird | first= Leslie | last= Nielsen | journal= Emu | volume= 64 | issue= 2 | page= 113 | year= 1964 | doi= 10.1071/MU964113 | url= http://www.publish.csiro.au/mu/pdf/MU964113 | url-access= subscription | archive-date= 2017-09-11 | access-date= 2017-09-11 | archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20170911115402/http://www.publish.csiro.au/mu/pdf/MU964113 | url-status= live }}</ref> Eggs of subspecies ''nigrogularis'' are larger, at around 33 mm long by 24 mm (1.3 by 0.95 in) wide, while those of subspecies ''picatus'' are around 31 mm long by 22 mm (1.2 by 0.85 in) wide. Incubation takes 19 to 21 days, with the eggs laid up to 48 hours apart and hatching at a similar interval. As with all [[passerine]]s, the chicks are [[altricial]]—they are born naked or sparsely covered in down and blind. They spend anywhere from 25 to 33 days in the nest before fledging, though they may leave the nest early if disturbed. They are fed by their parents and helper birds. Brood parasites recorded include the [[pallid cuckoo]] (''Cacomantis pallidus'') and [[channel-billed cuckoo]] (''Scythrops novaehollandiae'').{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=524}}<!-- cites previous 5 sentences -->

===Feeding===
The pied butcherbird is carnivorous and eats insects such as beetles, bugs, ants, caterpillars, and cockroaches, as well as spiders and worms. It [[predation|preys]] on vertebrates up to the size of animals such as frogs, skinks, mice, and small birds such as the [[silvereye]] (''Zosterops lateralis''), [[house sparrow]] (''Passer domesticus''), [[double-barred finch]] (''Taeniopygia bichenovii''), [[willie wagtail]] (''Rhipidura leucophrys''), and [[grey teal]] (''Anas gracilis'') duckling.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=520}} It has been looked upon favourably by farmers, since it hunts such pests as grasshoppers and rodents.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=519}} Some individuals look for scraps around houses and picnic sites and can become tame enough to be fed by people, either by hand or by tossing food in the air.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=520}} The pied butcherbird also eats fruit, such as those of sandpaper figs (''[[Ficus coronata]]''), native cherry (''[[Exocarpos cupressiformis]]''), African boxthorn (''[[Lycium ferocissimum]]''), and grapes (''[[Vitis vinifera]]''), and nectar of the Darwin woollybutt (''[[Eucalyptus miniata]]'').{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=520}}

The pied butcherbird often perches on a fencepost, stump, or branch while foraging for prey. It generally pounces on victims on the ground and eats them there. At times, it may hop or run along hunting ground-based food and occasionally seize flying insects. It typically forages alone or occasionally in pairs. The pied butcherbird has been observed hunting collaboratively with the [[Australian hobby]], either picking off [[common starling]]s or [[rufous-throated honeyeater]]s disturbed by the larger hobby or flushing out small birds from bushes, which the larger bird then hunts.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=520}}<!-- cites para --> The pied butcherbird sometimes stores food items by impaling them on a stick or on barbed wire or shoving them in a nook or crevice.{{sfn|Higgins|2006|p=520}}

==Cultural significance==
Several Australian and international composers have been inspired by and written music incorporating the songs of the pied butcherbird, including [[Henry Tate (poet)|Henry Tate]], [[David Lumsdaine]] (who described it as "a virtuoso of composition and improvisation"), [[Don Harper]], [[Olivier Messiaen]], [[Elaine Barkin]], [[John Rodgers (musician)|John Rodgers]], [[Ron Nagorcka]], and [[John Williamson (singer)|John Williamson]].<ref>{{cite journal|last=Taylor|first=Hollis|year=2011|title=Composers' appropriation of pied butcherbird song: Henry Tate's "undersong of Australia" comes of age|journal=Journal of Music Research Online|volume=2|pages=1–28|issn=1836-8336|url=http://www.jmro.org.au/index.php?journal=mca2&page=article&op=viewFile&path[]=43&path[]=21|access-date=18 December 2011|archive-date=11 October 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171011080330/http://www.jmro.org.au/index.php?journal=mca2&page=article&op=viewFile&path%5b%5d=43&path%5b%5d=21|url-status=live}}</ref><!-- if you cut and paste this ref it works fine - the brackets stuff the URL --> In the dance "Bird Song" by [[Siobhan Davies]], the main central solo was accompanied by the call of a pied butcherbird, and this same sound provided inspiration to much of the dance, including the improvisational aspects.<ref>{{cite news | title = Sound makes sense in new dance drama | url = http://www.scotsman.com/lifestyle/culture/sound-makes-sense-in-new-dance-drama-1-1009836 | newspaper = The Scotsman | date = 26 April 2004 | access-date = 8 July 2017 | archive-date = 5 September 2017 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20170905053336/http://www.scotsman.com/lifestyle/culture/sound-makes-sense-in-new-dance-drama-1-1009836 | url-status = live }}</ref> Composer and researcher Hollis Taylor has studied pied butcherbird songs for 12 years<ref>{{cite web | url=https://theconversation.com/birdsong-has-inspired-humans-for-centuries-is-it-music-79000 | title=Birdsong has inspired humans for centuries: is it music? | first=Hollis | last=Taylor | work=The Conversation | publisher=Conversation Media Group | date=26 July 2017 | access-date=23 September 2017 | archive-date=29 September 2017 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170929131301/http://theconversation.com/birdsong-has-inspired-humans-for-centuries-is-it-music-79000 | url-status=live }}</ref> and has released a double CD called ''Absolute Bird'' based on fifty-plus pied butcherbird nocturnal solo songs.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Taylor |first=Hollis |title=Absolute Bird |journal=ReR MEGACORP - RER HT1 | date=2017}}</ref> Taylor's "Is Birdsong Music? Outback Encounters with an Australian Songbird" offers portraits of the remote locations where the species is found.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Taylor |first=Hollis |year=2017 |title=Is Birdsong Music? Outback Encounters with an Australian Songbird |location=Bloomington, Indiana |publisher=Indiana University Press |isbn=978-0-253-02620-0}}</ref>

In the now extinct Warray language spoken on the Adelaide River in Arnhem Land, ''Cracticus nigrogularis'' was known as ''lopolopo''.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Ngoni Waray Amungal-Yang: The Waray Language from Adelaide River|last=Harvey|first=Mark|publisher=Australian National University|year=1986|page=299}}</ref>
